# Navigation and File Operations

Chapter 3 got you a working shell on your platform. Chapter 2
drew the map it runs on: one tree of files, a working
directory you are always standing in, and the difference
between an absolute path that names a place from the root and
a relative path read against wherever you happen to be. This
chapter is where you start to move. It is the daily loop every
terminal session is built from, walking through the tree,
looking at what is in it, and moving files around, made
operational on top of that model.

The commands are few and you will use them every day: `pwd`
and `ls` to see where you are and what is here, `cd` to move,
`mkdir` and `rmdir` to make and clear directories, and `cp`
and `mv` to copy and move files. What turns them from a list
into a skill is a single habit that runs through the chapter:
look before you leap. Copying and moving can overwrite an
existing file silently, with no prompt and no undo, so the
cheap insurance is to `ls` the target first and read what is
really there before you change it. The outright destructive
commands, `rm -rf` and clobbering a file with `>`, are
Chapter 7's subject; this chapter teaches the safe copy and
move habits and points there for the parts that cannot be
undone.

A note on where the output comes from. The portable commands
run in this book's Linux sandbox (Ubuntu 22.04.5, GNU bash
5.1.16, GNU coreutils 8.32; the `ch04-*.txt` transcript
files), in a clean copy of the asset-pricing project placed at
`/tmp/asset-pricing` and in throwaway `/tmp` scratch
directories for the demos that create and overwrite files.
This is also the first chapter to hit real BSD-versus-GNU
divergences on everyday commands, where the macOS tools behave
differently from the Linux baseline; those are captured on the
author's Mac (`transcripts/capture-ch04-mac.sh`, the
`ch04-*-mac.txt` files) and quoted where they bite. The
deliberate masks, as in Chapter 2: a home path reads
`/Users/[account]`, and because this chapter is the first to
show `ls -l`, whose owner and group columns print an account
name, that name is masked to `[account]` as well; the brackets
are a placeholder your own machine fills in. The Mac
transcripts carry one further scrub the chapter never displays
but the capture records: the macOS per-account temp directory
that `readlink -f` and `realpath` resolve paths into
(`/private/var/folders/<hash>/...`) has its identifying folder
hash masked to `/private/var/folders/[tmpdir]`.

## Where you are and what is here: `pwd` and `ls` {.tier-beginner}

Every session starts with the same two questions: where am I
standing, and what is in front of me. `pwd` (print working
directory) answers the first, and `ls` (list) answers the
second. Standing at the top of the asset-pricing project, the
pair sketches the whole layout:

```bash
$ pwd
/tmp/asset-pricing
$ ls
Makefile
README.md
data
output
pyproject.toml
report.qmd
scripts
```

`pwd` prints the absolute path of where the shell is standing,
the working directory Chapter 2 named. `ls` with no argument
lists that same directory: the project's files and its
subdirectories, sorted alphabetically. Give `ls` a path and it
lists *that* place instead, which is how you look into a
subdirectory without moving into it:

```bash
$ ls data/raw
factors.csv
firm_panel.csv
```

The two raw inputs the running example starts from are there:
`factors.csv` and `firm_panel.csv`. The argument `data/raw` is
a relative path, read against the working directory `pwd` just
printed, so this exact command depends on standing at the
project top. That silent precondition, the one Chapter 2
flagged behind "run `ls data/raw`," is the first thing the
next section makes concrete.

## Moving through the tree: `cd` {.tier-beginner}

`cd` (change directory) moves the shell to another place in
the tree. Give it a relative path and it moves down from where
you are; `..`, the special name for the parent directory,
moves back up. Walking into `data/raw` and back out shows both:

```bash
$ pwd
/tmp/asset-pricing
$ cd data/raw
$ pwd
/tmp/asset-pricing/data/raw
$ ls
factors.csv
firm_panel.csv
$ cd ..
$ pwd
/tmp/asset-pricing/data
```

`cd data/raw` followed a relative path two levels down; `cd
..` climbed one level back to `data`. An absolute path (one
starting with `/`) moves you to that exact place from anywhere,
no matter where you are standing, and `cd -` is a convenience
that jumps back to the directory you were in just before, the
way a "back" button toggles between two places:

```bash
$ cd /tmp/asset-pricing/output
$ pwd
/tmp/asset-pricing/output
$ cd -
/tmp/asset-pricing/data
$ pwd
/tmp/asset-pricing/data
```

The absolute `cd` jumped straight to `output` from an
unrelated directory; `cd -` then flipped back to `data` and
printed where it landed. One more shorthand completes the set:
`~` stands for your home directory, so `cd ~` (or just `cd`
with no argument) returns you home from anywhere. On the
author's Mac, home is under `/Users`:

```bash
$ echo $HOME
/Users/[account]

$ echo ~
/Users/[account]
```

A Linux server puts homes under `/home` instead, but the
shorthand is the same everywhere: `~` is your own corner of
the tree, and `cd ~` is how you get back to it.

One detail from Chapter 2 explains why `cd` behaves the way it
does. `cd` is a shell builtin, not a separate program, because
its whole job is to change *the shell's own* working
directory; an external program could only change its own and
then exit, leaving your shell where it started. That is why
there is no output on a successful `cd`: it quietly moves the
shell you are sitting in.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
A relative path means nothing without knowing where you are
standing, so the same command succeeds from one directory and
fails from another. Watch `ls data/raw` work from the project
top and then fail one directory away:

```bash
$ pwd
/tmp/asset-pricing
$ ls data/raw
factors.csv
firm_panel.csv
$ cd /tmp
$ ls data/raw
ls: cannot access 'data/raw': No such file or directory
```

Nothing about `data/raw` changed; only the working directory
did, and `/tmp` has no `data/raw` beneath it. This is the most
common cause of a "No such file or directory" error on a file
you can plainly see in a file-browser window: the shell is
standing somewhere else. `pwd` is the first thing to check
when a relative path fails, and running `pwd` (or `ls`) before
a command that matters is the habit that prevents it. When a
script must not depend on where it was launched from, it uses
absolute paths or `cd`s to a known directory first, a
discipline Chapter 11 will build into scripts that fail loudly.
:::

## Looking closely: `ls -l`, `-a`, and `stat` {.tier-beginner}

Bare `ls` gives names. Its options give detail, and three of
them earn a place in the daily loop. `-l` (long) prints one
file per line with its metadata; `-a` (all) includes hidden
files; and `-h` (human-readable), used with `-l`, prints sizes
in kilobytes and megabytes instead of raw bytes. Listing the
project the long way:

```bash
$ ls -l
total 28
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account] 2671 Jul  2 12:08 Makefile
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account] 3446 Jul  2 12:08 README.md
drwxr-xr-x 4 [account] [account] 4096 Jul  2 12:08 data
drwxr-xr-x 4 [account] [account] 4096 Jul  2 12:08 output
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account]  935 Jul  2 12:08 pyproject.toml
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account] 1556 Jul  2 12:08 report.qmd
drwxr-xr-x 2 [account] [account] 4096 Jul  2 12:08 scripts
```

Each line reads left to right: the first character is the type
(`-` for a regular file, `d` for a directory), the next nine
are the permissions (who may read, write, or execute it,
decoded just below), then the link count, the owner and group,
the size in bytes, the last-modified date, and the name. The
`data`,
`output`, and `scripts` lines start with `d`, so they are
directories; the rest are files. This is the view you reach
for constantly, because it answers "how big is this, when did
it change, and may I write to it" in one line.

The permission block repays a closer look, because you read it
constantly and it is less cryptic than it seems. The ten
characters are a type character followed by three triples.
Take `-rw-r--r--`: the leading `-` is the type (a regular
file), then `rw-` is the **owner** (may read and write, not
execute), `r--` is the **group** (read only), and the last
`r--` is **everyone else** (read only). Each triple is always
in the order read, write, execute, with a letter when the
permission is granted and a `-` when it is not. A directory
reads `drwxr-xr-x`: `d` for directory, then `rwx` for the
owner and `r-x` for group and others, where the execute bit on
a directory means permission to *enter* it (to `cd` in and
reach what is inside). That is enough to answer "may I change
this file, and who else can see it." Changing permissions with
`chmod`, the octal shorthand like `644` and `755`, and who
owns a file are Chapter 10's subject; here you only need to
read what `ls -l` already shows.

The `-a` option reveals what plain `ls` hides: names beginning
with a dot, the Unix convention for configuration and
housekeeping files that would otherwise clutter every listing:

```bash
$ ls -a
.
..
.gitignore
Makefile
README.md
data
output
pyproject.toml
report.qmd
scripts
```

Two of those entries are always present: `.` is the current
directory and `..` is the parent, the same `..` that `cd`
climbs. The `.gitignore` is a real hidden file, the one the
companion Git book relies on; you would never see it without
`-a`, which is exactly why a directory that looks empty to
plain `ls` may not be. Adding `-h` to a long listing makes the
sizes legible, which matters the moment a data file is large:

```bash
$ ls -lh data/raw
total 2.6M
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account]  12K Jul  2 12:08 factors.csv
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account] 2.6M Jul  2 12:08 firm_panel.csv
```

`12K` and `2.6M` are far easier to size up than `12046` and
`2684212`, and on a server where a stray file can fill a disk,
`ls -lh` is how you spot the offender.

When you want the full record for one file, `stat` prints
everything the filesystem knows about it:

```bash
$ stat data/raw/factors.csv
  File: data/raw/factors.csv
  Size: 11506     	Blocks: 24         IO Block: 4096   regular file
Device: 10302h/66306d	Inode: 34106       Links: 1
Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: ( 1169/[account])   Gid: ( 1169/[account])
Access: 2026-07-02 12:08:41.812001269 -0400
Modify: 2026-07-02 12:08:41.813001269 -0400
Change: 2026-07-02 12:09:44.604001299 -0400
 Birth: 2026-07-02 12:08:41.812001269 -0400
```

That is more than a daily listing needs, but the three
timestamps matter later: the modification time is what `make`
compares to decide whether a step needs rerunning (Chapter
12), so knowing how to read it by hand is worth the one
command. `stat` also takes a format string, `-c`, to print
just the fields you want:

```bash
$ stat -c '%n %s bytes, perms %A, modified %y' \
    data/raw/factors.csv
data/raw/factors.csv 11506 bytes, perms -rw-r--r--, modified 2026-07-02 12:08:41.813001269 -0400
```

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
`ls -l` looks nearly identical on macOS and Linux, but two
real differences hide in it. First, the `total` line at the
top is a count of disk blocks, and the block unit differs: GNU
`ls` (Linux) counts in 1-kilobyte blocks while BSD `ls`
(macOS) counts in 512-byte blocks, so the same directory
prints a different `total` on each platform (the macOS run in
`transcripts/ch04-ls-mac.txt` shows the smaller-unit count).
It is not a file count and never was, so do not read meaning
into the number matching across machines. Second, and nastier,
`-G` is a *false friend*: it does opposite jobs on the two
platforms. On GNU `ls` it suppresses the group column in a
long listing (`-G` is short for `--no-group`), so `ls -lG`
drops the second name:

```bash
$ ls -lG data/raw
total 2644
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account]   11506 Jul  2 12:08 factors.csv
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] 2695102 Jul  2 12:08 firm_panel.csv
```

On BSD `ls` (macOS) the same `-G` instead turns on colored
output, so the exact same `ls -lG` colorizes the listing and
keeps the group. Both platforms *do* accept `--color=when`
(macOS's own usage line lists it, as Chapter 2's captured
`ls` usage shows), so color is not the divergence; the trap is
that the short flag `-G` means one thing on your Mac and
another on the Linux server. This is Chapter 2's split in
miniature: macOS ships the BSD tools, so the same flag can do
a different job, and `--help` (a GNU habit) still fails there.
:::

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
`stat` is the sharper split, because the two versions disagree
on the flag *and* the format language. GNU `stat` (Linux) uses
`-c` with `%s` for size, `%n` for name, `%y` for modification
time, as shown above. BSD `stat` (macOS) uses `-f` with a
different set of specifiers, `%z` for size, `%N` for name,
`%Sm` for the modification time, so the portable-looking `stat
-c '%s'` prints nothing useful on a Mac. The macOS equivalent
of the format above, captured on a two-byte scratch file in
`transcripts/ch04-stat-mac.txt`, produces the same kind of
line from an entirely different flag and syntax:

```bash
$ stat -f '%N %z bytes, perms %Sp, modified %Sm' \
    proj/data/raw/factors.csv
proj/data/raw/factors.csv 2 bytes, perms -rw-r--r--, modified Jul  2 13:45:42 2026
```

When a script needs one file's size or timestamp portably, the
honest options are to branch on the platform or to reach for a
tool that behaves the same everywhere (`wc -c` for a byte
count, or Python's `os.stat`); do not assume one `stat`
invocation runs on both.
:::

## Making and clearing directories: `mkdir` and `rmdir` {.tier-beginner}

`mkdir` (make directory) creates a directory; `rmdir` (remove
directory) removes an empty one. The wrinkle worth learning on
day one is what happens when you ask for a nested path whose
middle pieces do not exist yet. Plain `mkdir` refuses, because
it makes exactly one directory:

```bash
$ mkdir results
$ mkdir results/2026/figures
mkdir: cannot create directory ‘results/2026/figures’: No such file or directory
$ echo $?
1
```

`results` was created, but `results/2026/figures` failed:
there was no `results/2026` for `figures` to go inside, and
`mkdir` will not invent the missing parent. The `-p` (parents)
option fixes exactly this, creating every directory in the
chain that does not yet exist, and staying silent if the whole
path is already there:

```bash
$ mkdir -p results/2026/figures
$ echo $?
0
$ ls -R results
results:
2026

results/2026:
figures

results/2026/figures:
```

`ls -R` (recursive) walks the tree it built: `results`
contains `2026`, which contains `figures`. Because `mkdir -p`
does not complain when a directory already exists, it is the
form you want in scripts, where a run should not fail just
because a folder is already there. Chapter 6 will lean on it to
scaffold the whole project layout in one line.

`rmdir` is the deliberately safe way to remove a directory: it
removes one *only if it is empty*, and refuses otherwise. It
cannot, by design, delete your files:

```bash
$ rmdir results/2026/figures
$ rmdir results
rmdir: failed to remove 'results': Directory not empty
```

Removing the empty `figures` succeeded; removing `results`
failed, because `results/2026` was still inside it. That
refusal is a feature, not an obstacle. Clearing out a
directory that still has contents means removing those contents
first, and doing *that* recursively in one command is `rm -r`,
which has no such guard rail and no undo. That command, and the
care it demands, is Chapter 7's territory; `rmdir` is the tool
for the safe, everyday case of tidying up an empty folder.

## Copying and moving: `cp` and `mv` {.tier-beginner}

`cp` (copy) duplicates a file; `mv` (move) relocates one, and
because relocating within a directory is the same as renaming,
`mv` is also how you rename. Duplicating a raw input and
checking the result:

```bash
$ ls data/raw
factors.csv
$ cp data/raw/factors.csv data/raw/factors_backup.csv
$ ls data/raw
factors.csv
factors_backup.csv
```

`cp` on its own copies a single file. To copy a whole
directory and everything under it, add `-r` (recursive), the
same recursive idea as `ls -R`:

```bash
$ cp -r data data_snapshot
$ ls data_snapshot/raw
factors.csv
factors_backup.csv
```

`mv` both renames and relocates. Renaming is a move whose
destination is a new name in the same directory; relocating is
a move whose destination is another directory:

```bash
$ mv data/raw/factors_backup.csv data/raw/factors_2026.csv
$ ls data/raw
factors.csv
factors_2026.csv
$ mv data/raw/factors_2026.csv archive/
$ ls archive
factors_2026.csv
```

The first `mv` renamed the backup in place; the second moved it
into `archive/`, leaving `data/raw` with just the original.
Both commands did their work silently, which is convenient
until the destination already exists. Then the quiet becomes
dangerous: copying or moving onto an existing file **replaces
it**, with no prompt and no copy left behind.

```bash
$ printf 'keep me\n' > results.csv
$ printf 'different\n' > new.csv
$ ls results.csv
results.csv
$ cp new.csv results.csv
$ cat results.csv
different
```

`results.csv` held `keep me`; after one `cp` it holds
`different`, and the original is gone. This is where the
chapter's habit earns its keep: look before you leap. The
`ls results.csv` above already told you the target existed, so
the overwrite should have been no surprise. When you would
rather the shell ask, the `-i` (interactive) option makes `cp`
and `mv` prompt before replacing anything. Recreate a
`keep me` file first, in the same scratch, so the contrast is
clean:

```bash
$ printf 'keep me\n' > results.csv
$ cp -i new.csv results.csv
cp: overwrite 'results.csv'?
$ cat results.csv
keep me
```

The prompt was answered `n` here (type `n` and press return to
leave the file alone, `y` to replace it), so `results.csv`
still reads `keep me`, where the plain `cp` a moment ago
replaced it without asking. Many people alias `cp` and `mv` to
their `-i` forms in their shell startup file (Chapter 18) so
the prompt is always on.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
`cp` and `mv` overwrite an existing destination silently and
irreversibly. There is no trash folder and no undo: the moment
`cp new.csv results.csv` runs, the old `results.csv` is gone.
This is the same hazard `>` carries in Chapter 7, which owns
the destructive commands in full (`rm -rf`, clobbering with
`>`); the point here is that a plain copy or move belongs on
the same watch list. Three habits contain it: `ls` the
destination before you copy or move onto it, so you know
whether you are about to replace something; use `cp -i` / `mv
-i` (or their aliases) when you want the shell to stop and ask;
and never point a copy or move at a path built from an unset
variable, the empty-variable trap Chapter 7 guards with
`${VAR:?}`. Appendix A collects the recovery options for when
one of these gets past you.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
If you have just overwritten a file with `cp` or `mv`, the
original is not in a trash folder, so recovery depends entirely
on what you set up beforehand. If the file was committed, the
companion Git book's `git restore` brings it back, which is one
more reason to commit early and often. If it was a generated
artifact, regenerate it: on the asset-pricing example a
clobbered `output/` file rebuilds from the seeded scripts with
one `make` (Chapter 12), which is the whole point of making
outputs reproducible rather than precious. For an untracked,
unbacked-up raw file, there may be no recovery at all, the same
hard truth Chapter 7 draws for `rm`, and the reason the
look-before-you-leap habit is cheaper than the alternative.
:::

## Symbolic links: `ln -s` {.tier-advanced}

A **symbolic link** (symlink) is a small file that points at
another path by name, a stable alias for something whose real
location or version changes. `ln -s` creates one, naming the
target first and the link second:

```bash
$ ln -s data/raw/factors.csv latest_factors.csv
$ ls -l latest_factors.csv
lrwxrwxrwx 1 [account] [account] 20 Jul  2 12:10 latest_factors.csv -> data/raw/factors.csv
```

The `ls -l` line marks it plainly: the type character is `l`
(link), and the name is printed as `latest_factors.csv ->
data/raw/factors.csv`, the arrow showing what it points at.
For a researcher the everyday use is a stable name in front of
a moving target: a `latest/` link that always points at the
newest dated results folder, or a link to a large dataset that
lives outside the repository so scripts can open a predictable
path without copying gigabytes. `readlink` prints the target a
link stores:

```bash
$ readlink latest_factors.csv
data/raw/factors.csv
```

To resolve a link all the way to the absolute path of the real
file, GNU offers `readlink -f` and `realpath`, which agree:

```bash
$ readlink -f latest_factors.csv
/tmp/asset-pricing/data/raw/factors.csv
$ realpath latest_factors.csv
/tmp/asset-pricing/data/raw/factors.csv
```

One choice, independent of platform, decides how well a link
travels: whether its stored target is relative or absolute.
The link above stores a *relative* target,
`data/raw/factors.csv`, which is resolved against the
directory the link sits in, not against wherever you happen to
be standing. That has a useful consequence for research work:
if you move or copy the whole project tree, link and target
travel together and the relative path still points at the
right file, on your laptop or on a server whose paths look
nothing like yours. It breaks only if you move the link *by
itself*, away from the directory that gives its relative path
meaning. An *absolute* target (`ln -s
/Users/you/proj/data/raw/factors.csv ...`) is the opposite
trade: it survives moving the link on its own, but breaks the
moment the project lives at a different path, which is exactly
what happens when the same repository is checked out on
another machine. For a project meant to move between machines,
the book's whole premise, a relative target inside the tree is
usually the more portable choice; reach for an absolute target
only when the link must point at something fixed and outside
the project (a shared dataset at a stable system path).

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
Creating a link with `ln -s` and reading it with plain
`readlink` behave the same on macOS and Linux. Fully
*resolving* a link to an absolute path is where the history
gets uneven. `readlink -f` and `realpath` began as GNU
features, and older macOS shipped neither a `readlink` that
took `-f` nor a `realpath` at all. Recent macOS has caught up:
on the author's Mac (macOS 26.5.1) both `readlink -f` and
`realpath` resolve the link exactly as GNU does, exit status
zero, recorded in `transcripts/ch04-symlink-mac.txt`. The
catch is that "recent" is doing real work in that sentence: a
script that must also run on an older Mac, or on a bare BSD,
cannot assume either flag exists. So the portable habit for
anything meant to travel is to rely only on plain `readlink`
(the target as stored) and, when you truly need an absolute
path, to resolve it in a way you know works everywhere (a `cd`
into the directory followed by `pwd` is crude but portable),
rather than to lean on `readlink -f` or `realpath` and hope
the target machine is new enough.
:::

## When names collide: case sensitivity {.tier-advanced}

Whether `Foo.txt` and `foo.txt` are two files or one is a
property of the *filesystem*, not the shell, and it is one of
the sharpest quiet differences between a Mac and a Linux
server. On Linux, filenames are case-sensitive, so the two are
distinct:

```bash
$ touch Foo.txt
$ touch foo.txt
$ ls
Foo.txt
foo.txt
$ printf 'upper\n' > Foo.txt
$ printf 'lower\n' > foo.txt
$ cat Foo.txt
upper
$ cat foo.txt
lower
```

Two names, two files, two different contents. The default
macOS filesystem (APFS) is case-*insensitive* but
case-*preserving*: it remembers the capitalization you typed
but treats names that differ only in case as the **same file**,
so the same sequence there produces one file, and writing
`foo.txt` overwrites `Foo.txt`. That side is captured on the
Mac in `transcripts/ch04-case-mac.txt`.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
This bites in one direction and it is the expensive one. A
project created on a case-insensitive Mac can contain a script
that opens `data/Panel.csv` while the file on disk is
`panel.csv`, and it works fine on the Mac because the two names
collide into one. Move that project to a case-sensitive Linux
server, the usual remote-compute target, and the same script
fails with "No such file or directory," because now the case
must match exactly. It can also surprise Git: two files that
differ only in case are distinct in a repository built on
Linux but cannot coexist in a working copy checked out on a
Mac. The defense is a convention, not a command: pick one
casing for names (lowercase is the common choice) and use it
consistently, so your code never depends on a case-folding
your server will not do. Chapter 11 will return to this
alongside the locale and line-ending settings that similarly
shift behavior between machines.
:::

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

These use the asset-pricing project from your copy of the
book's repository. Items 1 to 3 only look and destroy nothing,
so run them in the project itself. Items 4 to 6 create files,
directories, and links, so make a throwaway copy under `/tmp`
first and work only in there, never in your real project. The
setup is the same two commands each time, with your own project
path in place of the placeholder:

```text
# illustrative setup (placeholder path; not a captured run):
cp -r <your-project> /tmp/ch04-play
cd /tmp/ch04-play
# then do items 4 to 6 in there, not in your real project
```

When you are done you can delete the whole `/tmp/ch04-play`
copy; removing a directory tree is `rm -r`, which Chapter 7
covers along with the care it demands, and `/tmp` is cleared
on reboot in any case.

1. Orient yourself. From the project top, run `pwd`, then `ls`
   and `ls -l`. Read one line of the `ls -l` output aloud:
   name the type character, say whether the owner may write to
   it, and read its size and modification date. Then `ls -lh`
   the two files in `data/raw` and note which is larger.

2. Walk the tree with `cd`. Move into `data/raw`, confirm with
   `pwd`, list it, then `cd ..` back to `data`. Jump to
   `output` with an absolute path, use `cd -` to flip back, and
   finish with `cd ~` to go home. Predict each `pwd` before you
   run it.

3. Trip the working-directory pitfall on purpose. From the
   project top, run `ls data/raw` and watch it succeed; `cd`
   to your home directory and run the identical command; read
   the error and explain, in one sentence, why the same
   command now fails.

4. Build and clear a directory. Make `output/scratch/tmp` in
   one command with `mkdir -p`, confirm with `ls -R output`,
   then remove the two directories you made with `rmdir` from
   the bottom up. Try `rmdir` on a directory that still has
   something in it and read the refusal.

5. Copy, then clobber, in a throwaway copy. Copy `data` to
   `data_copy` with `cp -r`. Inside it, `cp` one file onto
   another existing file and confirm with `cat` that the
   target was silently replaced. Redo it with `cp -i` and
   decline the prompt. Say in one sentence what would have
   brought the file back if it had been committed to Git.

6. Make a symlink and resolve it, inside `/tmp/ch04-play`.
   Create `latest_factors.csv` pointing at
   `data/raw/factors.csv` with `ln -s`, inspect it with `ls -l`
   and `readlink`, then run `readlink -f` and `realpath` and
   note whether each resolves on your platform (on a recent Mac
   both do; on an older one they may not). Then create `Foo.txt`
   and `foo.txt` and check whether `ls` shows one file or two,
   which tells you whether your filesystem is case-sensitive.
